Day3: TypeScript in 30 Days
Learn TypeScript functions and arrow functions with practical examples. Understand parameters, return types, optional parameters, default values, rest parameters, and best practices used in real-world projects.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by AuthorIntroduction
In the previous two days, we learned how to describe data using TypeScript with variables, types, arrays, objects, and interfaces.
If you missed —
But data alone doesn’t make an application useful.
If variables and objects are the building blocks of TypeScript, then functions are where the real action happens.
Every backend API, every frontend button click, every data transformation… it all runs through functions.
But here’s the thing.
In TypeScript, functions are not just about writing logic. They’re about defining how data flows safely from one place to another.
Today we’ll break it down in a simple way:

• What functions are

• How to write them in TypeScript

• Arrow functions vs normal functions

• Parameters and return types

• Optional, default, and rest parameters

• Real-world usage patterns
Let’s get into it.

What is a Function?
A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task.
Think of it like a machine.
Coffee Machine Example
You give inputs like water, coffee powder, sugar.
It gives output as coffee.
Same with functions:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by Author
ATM Example
You insert a card and enter amount.
ATM processes it and gives cash.
You don’t care how it works internally. You just use it.
That’s exactly what functions do in programming.

Basic Function in TypeScript
function greet(name: string): string {
return `Hello, ${name}`;
}Usage
console.log(greet("Sanajit"));What’s happening here

• name: string → input must be a string

• : string after function → return type is string

• TypeScript ensures both are correct
If you try:
greet(123);TypeScript immediately throws an error.

Function Return Types
You can explicitly define what a function returns.
Number example
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
return a + b;
}Boolean example
function isEven(num: number): boolean {
return num % 2 === 0;
}This makes your code predictable and safer.

Function Without Return (void)
Sometimes a function doesn’t return anything.
function logMessage(message: string): void {
console.log(message);
}void simply means: nothing is returned.

Optional Parameters
Sometimes you don’t always want to pass all values.
function greetUser(name: string, age?: number): string {
return age
? `Hello ${name}, age ${age}`
: `Hello ${name}`;
}Usage
greetUser("Amit");
greetUser("Amit", 25);The ? makes the parameter optional.

Default Parameters
You can also set default values.
function multiply(a: number, b: number = 2): number {
return a * b;
}Usage
multiply(5); // 10
multiply(5, 3); // 15If no value is passed, default is used.

Rest Parameters
This is used when you don’t know how many values will come.
function sum(...numbers: number[]): number {
return numbers.reduce((acc, num) => acc + num, 0);
}Usage
sum(1, 2, 3);
sum(10, 20, 30, 40);Think of it like collecting all inputs into a single array.

Function Expression
Functions can also be stored in variables.
const subtract = function (a: number, b: number): number {
return a - b;
};Same behavior, different style.

Arrow Functions in TypeScript
Now comes the modern way.
Arrow functions are shorter, cleaner, and widely used in real projects.

Basic Arrow Function
const greet = (name: string): string => {
return `Hello, ${name}`;
};
Shorter Version
If there’s only one line:
const square = (num: number): number => num * num;No need for return keyword.

Arrow Function with Multiple Parameters
const add = (a: number, b: number): number => a + b;Simple and clean.

Why Arrow Functions are Popular
Here’s what this really means in real projects:
1. Less boilerplate
Less code, same output.
2. Common in React and Node.js
You’ll see this everywhere:
const getUser = (id: number) => fetchUser(id);3. No own this
Arrow functions don’t create their own this, which avoids a lot of bugs in callbacks.

Real World Example
Imagine a user service in a backend system.
Normal function
function getUserById(id: number): string {
return `User with id ${id}`;
}Arrow function version
const getUserById = (id: number): string => {
return `User with id ${id}`;
};In modern codebases, arrow functions are preferred for this style.

Array Functions (Very Important)
Functions are heavily used with arrays.
Map Example
const numbers: number[] = [1, 2, 3];const squared = numbers.map((num: number) => num * num);

Filter Example
const evenNumbers = numbers.filter((num: number) => num % 2 === 0);
Reduce Example
const total = numbers.reduce((acc: number, num: number) => acc + num, 0);This is where TypeScript functions become extremely powerful in real projects.

Common Mistakes
Missing return type clarity
function add(a: number, b: number) {
return a + b;
}Works, but in large systems it’s better to be explicit.

Wrong parameter type
const greet = (name: string) => {
return `Hello ${name}`;
};greet(100);
❌ Error

Forgetting optional handling
function printName(name?: string) {
return name.toUpperCase();
}❌ Error because name might be undefined

Best Practices
✔ Use arrow functions for most modern code
✔ Always define parameter types in public APIs
✔ Use void when nothing is returned
✔ Prefer optional parameters over undefined checks
✔ Keep functions small and focused

Interview Questions
What is the difference between a normal function and an arrow function?
Arrow functions are shorter, don’t have their own this, and are widely used in modern JavaScript and TypeScript.
What is a rest parameter?
It allows a function to accept multiple values as an array.
Why use TypeScript with functions?
It ensures correct input and output types, preventing runtime errors.

Key Takeaways

• Functions are reusable logic blocks

• TypeScript adds type safety to inputs and outputs

• Optional, default, and rest parameters make functions flexible

• Arrow functions are modern and widely used

• Real-world apps heavily rely on function-based patterns

What’s Next?
In Day 4, we’ll go deeper into:
Union Types, Type Aliases, and Enums
This is where TypeScript starts feeling like a real engineering tool instead of just “JavaScript with types”.
We’ll also see how large systems manage complex data structures cleanly.

I’m publishing one TypeScript article every day for the next 30 days, covering everything from fundamentals to advanced concepts used in real production systems.
If you found this article helpful, please give it a clap 👏, drop a comment and share it with friends who may find it useful, and follow for more updates.
Thanks for reading -see you in the next story! 💗
